| * [v](/wiki/Template:Transliteration_of_Chinese "Template:Transliteration of Chinese") * [t](/wiki/Template_talk:Transliteration_of_Chinese "Template talk:Transliteration of Chinese") * [e](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Template:Transliteration_of_Chinese "Special:EditPage/Template:Transliteration of Chinese") |

[Personal names](/wiki/Personal_name "Personal name") in [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong "Hong Kong") reflect the co-official status of [Cantonese](/wiki/Cantonese "Cantonese") and [English](/wiki/English_language "English language") in Hong Kong. A total of 25.8% of [Hongkongers](/wiki/Hongkongers "Hongkongers") have English given names as part of their legal names; a further 38.3% of Hongkongers go by English given names even though those are not part of their legal names. The two figures add up to a total of 64.1% of Hongkongers having English names, according to a survey of 2049 respondents in 2015.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

For example, a person who has Kuo as his surname, Chi Yung as his Cantonese given name and Peter as his legal English given name, would have his name rendered as either "KUO Chi Yung Peter" or "KUO Chi Yung, Peter" (with [comma](/wiki/Comma "Comma")) on court papers,[[2]](#cite_note-HKLR_383-2) in the format of "Surname + Cantonese given name + optional [comma](/wiki/Comma "Comma") + English given name", though non-court commentaries might render his name as Peter Kuo Chi-yung (with [hyphen](/wiki/Hyphen "Hyphen")) or Peter Kuo.[[3]](#cite_note-HKLJ-3)

## Surname

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hongkonger_name&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Surname")]

Generally, the Cantonese majority employ one or another [romanization of Cantonese](/wiki/Romanization_of_Cantonese "Romanization of Cantonese").[[4]](#cite_note-4) However, non-Cantonese immigrants may retain their hometown spelling in English. For example, use of [Shanghainese](/wiki/Shanghainese "Shanghainese") romanization in names (e.g. [Joseph Zen Ze-kiun](/wiki/Joseph_Zen_Ze-kiun "Joseph Zen Ze-kiun")) is more common in [Hong Kong English](/wiki/Hong_Kong_English "Hong Kong English") than in official use in [Shanghai](/wiki/Shanghai "Shanghai") where [Mandarin](/wiki/Standard_Chinese "Standard Chinese")-based [pinyin](/wiki/Pinyin "Pinyin") has been in official use since the 1950s.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

## Given name

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hongkonger_name&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Given name")]

[Chinese names](/wiki/Chinese_name "Chinese name") and sometimes [Chinese surnames](/wiki/Chinese_surname "Chinese surname") in Hong Kong may be supplemented by or replaced by an English name when using English. The use of English names in Hong Kong is not well researched or documented.[[6]](#cite_note-6) English names in Hong Kong can use various proper names and nouns that are not often found in the [Western world](/wiki/Western_world "Western world"), with some examples being [Rimsky Yuen](/wiki/Rimsky_Yuen "Rimsky Yuen"), [York Chow](/wiki/York_Chow "York Chow"), and [Moses Chan](/wiki/Moses_Chan "Moses Chan"). Inspiration for English names in Hong Kong can come from the names of months, sports brands, and luxury labels. More conventional English names can undergo distortion by the addition, substitution, or deletion of letters (e.g. Sonija, Garbie, Kith), as well using suffixes like *-son* (e.g. Rayson). Others adopt a Western name that sounds similar phonetically to their Chinese name, such as [Hacken Lee](/wiki/Hacken_Lee "Hacken Lee") from Lee Hak-kan (李克勤).[[7]](#cite_note-man-7) These categories (addition, substitution, phonetic-based, etc.) are the fundamental ways of generating creative Hong Kong names.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

## Maiden name

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hongkonger_name&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Maiden name")]

In case a married person uses the spouse's surname, the [maiden name](/wiki/Maiden_name "Maiden name") is usually placed after that surname. For instance, the 4th Chief Executive of Hong Kong [Carrie Lam Cheng Yuet-ngor](/wiki/Carrie_Lam_Cheng_Yuet-ngor "Carrie Lam Cheng Yuet-ngor")[[9]](#cite_note-9) has Carrie and Yuet-ngor as the English and Cantonese personal names respectively, Cheng as the maiden name, and Lam from the surname of her husband [Lam Siu-por](/wiki/Lam_Siu-por "Lam Siu-por"). In the English-speaking world, married women are sometimes referred by "first name + married name" style, which is Carrie Lam in the example above,[[10]](#cite_note-10) while locally as "married name + maiden name + personal name" style.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

## See also

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hongkonger_name&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: See also")]

* [List of Hong Kong placename etymologies](/wiki/List_of_Hong_Kong_placename_etymologies "List of Hong Kong placename etymologies")
* [Hongkongers](/wiki/Hongkongers "Hongkongers")
* [Culture of Hong Kong](/wiki/Culture_of_Hong_Kong "Culture of Hong Kong")
* [Official Cantonese translations of English names for British officials](/wiki/Official_Cantonese_translations_of_English_names_for_British_officials "Official Cantonese translations of English names for British officials")

## References

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hongkonger_name&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: References")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-1)** Bacon-Shone, John; Bolton, Kingsley; Luke, Kang Kwong (2015). [*Language use, proficiency and attitudes in Hong Kong*](http://www.ssrc.hku.hk/files/language_2/HK_lg_survey_Final.pdf) (PDF). Hong Kong: Social Sciences Research Centre, The University of Hong Kong. p. 29.
2. **[^](#cite_ref-HKLR_383_2-0)** Dataprep (HK) Ltd v. KUO Chi Yung Peter [1974], *Hong Kong Law Reports* 383.
3. **[^](#cite_ref-HKLJ_3-0)** "Transfers from the Labour Tribunal and Procedural Problems in the Supreme Court - Dataprep (H.K.) Ltd. v. Peter Kuo Chi-yung". *Hong Kong Law Journal*. **5**. 1975.
4. **[^](#cite_ref-4)** Caroline Courtauld, May Holdsworth, Simon Vickers The Hong Kong story -1997 Page xi "A Note on the Romanization of Chinese Names The majority of Chinese personal names in Hong Kong are romanized according to their Cantonese pronunciation; a small number are rendered differently. In all cases we have retained the ..."
